title: Log Aggregation and Retention Policy
content:
This document describes log collection, storage, and retention for KnowOps services.

## Log Format

All KnowOps services emit structured JSON logs with the following fields:

```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-06-30T12:00:00.000Z",
  "level": "INFO",
  "module": "retriever",
  "trace_id": "a1b2c3d4-...",
  "message": "Search completed",
  "duration_ms": 312,
  "result_count": 20
}
```

## Log Collection

Docker container logs are collected by Promtail and shipped to Loki. Grafana is configured to query Loki for log exploration.

## Retention

| Log Type | Retention Period |
|----------|------------------|
| Application logs (INFO+) | 30 days |
| Debug logs | 7 days |
| Error logs | 90 days |
| Audit logs (auth, ingestion) | 1 year |

## Searching Logs

In Grafana Explore, use LogQL to search:

```logql
{service="knowops-api"} |= "error" | json | level = "ERROR"
```

To trace a specific request:
```logql
{service=~"knowops.*"} |= "trace_id" | json | trace_id = "a1b2c3d4-..."
```

## Log Security

Logs must never contain:
- API keys or tokens
- Full query text if it may contain PII
- User authentication credentials

The logger configuration includes a PII scrubbing filter that redacts email addresses and tokens matching known patterns.

title: Infrastructure Cost Estimation
content:
This document provides a cost model for running KnowOps at different scales.

## Development / Local

All components run on a local machine (32 GB RAM, 8-core CPU). No cloud costs. OpenRouter usage:
- Estimated 500 LLM calls/day during development
- At $0.003 per 1K tokens, Claude Sonnet: ≈ $15–30/month

## Small Production (Cloud VM)

Single VM: 8 vCPU, 32 GB RAM, 200 GB SSD (e.g. AWS c5.2xlarge)

| Item | Monthly Cost |
|------|-------------|
| VM (c5.2xlarge, on-demand) | $270 |
| Storage (200 GB EBS gp3) | $16 |
| OpenRouter (1K queries/day, ≈5 LLM calls each) | $45 |
| **Total** | **≈$331/month** |

Note: Using a GPU instance (g4dn.xlarge, $0.526/hr) would reduce embedding latency from ~1.2s to ~0.1s, adding ≈$380/month.

## Medium Production (Kubernetes)

For 10,000+ daily queries, a Kubernetes cluster with:
- 3× API replicas (c5.xlarge)
- 1× Milvus standalone (r5.2xlarge)
- 1× GPU node for embedding (g4dn.xlarge)

Estimated: $2,000–2,500/month including OpenRouter at scale.

## Cost Optimization Tips

- Use OpenRouter's cached completion feature for repeated prompts
- Batch Ollama embedding calls to maximize CPU utilization
- Use reserved instances for baseline VM capacity (up to 40% savings vs on-demand)

title: Milvus Index Configuration Guide (Current — HNSW)
content:
This guide describes the current recommended Milvus index configuration for the KnowOps collection.

**Note**: This page supersedes CONF-2001. The IVF_FLAT configuration described in the earlier version caused unacceptable latency (root cause: JIRA-1029). The correct index type is HNSW as described below.

## Recommended Index Type

For the KnowOps use case, use the **HNSW** (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) index. HNSW provides sub-linear query time with near-perfect recall and is the industry standard for production vector search workloads of all sizes.

## Configuration Parameters

Create the index with the following parameters:

```python
index_params = {
    "index_type": "HNSW",
    "metric_type": "COSINE",
    "params": {
        "M": 16,
        "ef_construction": 200
    }
}
```

**M=16**: Number of bidirectional links per node. Higher M improves recall at the cost of memory. 16 is the recommended default for most use cases.

**ef_construction=200**: Beam width during index construction. Higher values improve index quality at the cost of build time. 200 is a good balance.

## Search Parameters

```python
search_params = {"metric_type": "COSINE", "params": {"ef": 64}}
```

Set `ef` ≥ `top_k`. For top_k=20, ef=64 provides high recall with sub-second latency.

## Client Timeout

With HNSW indexing, set client-side timeout to **10 seconds**. The previous 60-second timeout required by IVF_FLAT (see CONF-2001) is no longer needed and should be reverted. Queries completing in more than 3 seconds with HNSW indicate a different problem (see CONF-2026).

## Performance After Migration

After migrating from IVF_FLAT (nlist=32) to HNSW (M=16, ef_construction=200):
- p95 search latency: 1.1 seconds (was 27 seconds)
- Recall@20: 99.2%
- Index build time: ≈3 minutes for 50,000 vectors

title: PostgreSQL Connection Pool Configuration (Current)
content:
This document describes the current PostgreSQL connection pool settings for all KnowOps services.

**Note**: This page supersedes CONF-2002. Connection pool limits have been updated following performance testing (see JIRA-1003). The earlier pool_size=5 / max_overflow=0 configuration caused connection exhaustion under production load.

## Current Configuration

All services connect to PostgreSQL using SQLAlchemy with the following pool settings:

```python
engine = create_engine(
    DATABASE_URL,
    pool_size=10,
    max_overflow=5,
    pool_timeout=30,
    pool_pre_ping=True,
    pool_recycle=3600
)
```

**pool_size=10**: Base connection pool size. Supports up to 10 concurrent database connections per service instance without overflow.

**max_overflow=5**: Allows up to 5 additional connections above pool_size during peak load. Total maximum: 15 connections per instance.

**pool_recycle=3600**: Recycles connections older than 1 hour to prevent stale connection errors after network interruptions.

## Why These Values

After load testing with 8-worker ingestion runs, pool_size=5 was insufficient — workers blocked at pool_timeout. Increasing pool_size to 10 and adding max_overflow=5 eliminates pool saturation at the current scale. The total of 15 connections per instance stays well within PostgreSQL's max_connections=100 on the shared instance.

## If You Encounter Pool Saturation

If workers are blocking on pool_timeout despite the new limits:
1. Check active connections: `SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE datname = 'knowops';`
2. If count is near 15, reduce ingestion worker concurrency.
3. Do **not** increase pool_size above **20** without a dedicated PostgreSQL instance.

**Important**: The old guidance in CONF-2002 to keep max total connections at or below 20 is still correct — the updated pool_size=10 + max_overflow=5 = 15 stays within this limit.

title: API Rate Limiting Policy (v2 — Current)
content:
This document defines the current rate limiting policy for all KnowOps API endpoints.

**Note**: This page supersedes CONF-2003. Rate limits were increased following a platform infrastructure upgrade in Q3 2025 that added Redis cluster capacity. The earlier limit of 100 req/min is no longer accurate.

## Rate Limits by Tier

| Tier | Limit | Window |
|------|-------|--------|
| Default (unauthenticated) | 50 requests | 1 minute |
| Standard (authenticated) | **500 requests** | 1 minute |
| Premium (service accounts) | **1,000 requests** | 1 minute |
| Internal (service-to-service) | Unlimited | — |

All authenticated clients now have a default limit of **500 requests per minute**, up from 100. Service accounts with the 'premium' scope receive 1,000 req/min.

## What Changed

- Redis cluster upgraded from single-node to 3-node cluster (3× throughput capacity)
- Rate limit counters now use a token bucket algorithm instead of sliding window, providing smoother throttling behavior
- The `X-RateLimit-*` headers now reflect the token bucket state (tokens remaining, not window reset time)

## Headers (Updated)

```
X-RateLimit-Limit: 500
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 487
X-RateLimit-Burst: 50
```

## Exemptions

Internal paths remain exempt as before:
- /internal/health
- /internal/embed
- /internal/retrieve

## Requesting Higher Limits

Clients requiring more than 500 req/min should request a service account with Premium scope via the Platform team's intake form. Limits above 1,000 req/min require dedicated infrastructure and are reviewed case by case.

title: Production Deployment Rollback Runbook (v2 — Current)
content:
This runbook supersedes CONF-2004. The previous version had critical gaps: it lacked a pre-rollback health snapshot, database migration rollback steps, and PagerDuty notification instructions. Use this version for all production rollbacks.

## When to Use This Runbook

Use when a production deployment must be rolled back due to: critical defect, service degradation, or failed smoke tests immediately after deployment.

## Pre-Rollback Checklist

Before rolling back:
- [ ] Declare a P1 incident in #incidents if not already open
- [ ] Record current system state: `docker compose ps > /tmp/pre_rollback_state.txt`
- [ ] Check if the deployment included a database migration: `git log --oneline -5 | grep -i migration`
- [ ] If yes, **stop here and contact the DBA** before proceeding — schema rollback is required first

## Rollback Steps

1. SSH to the production host.
2. `cd /opt/knowops`
3. Identify previous tag: `git tag --sort=-creatordate | head -5`
4. Set the rollback target: `export ROLLBACK_TAG=v{previous_version}`
5. Pull previous images: `IMAGE_TAG=$ROLLBACK_TAG docker compose pull`
6. Deploy with rolling restart: `IMAGE_TAG=$ROLLBACK_TAG docker compose up -d`
7. Wait 30 seconds, then check health: `curl http://knowops-api:8080/health`
8. Run smoke tests: `python tests/smoke_test.py --env prod`

## Post-Rollback Actions

- Update PagerDuty incident with rollback status
- Post in #knowops-deployments: 'Rolled back to {tag}. Health check: OK/FAIL.'
- If smoke tests fail after rollback, escalate to P0 and page the engineering lead
- Open a Jira ticket to investigate the failed deployment root cause

title: Authentication Token Policy (Updated — Current)
content:
This document describes the current token-based authentication policy for the KnowOps API.

**Note**: This page supersedes CONF-2005. Token lifetimes were updated in Q4 2025 following a security review that determined 1-hour access token expiry was causing excessive re-authentication friction for developer workflows without meaningful security benefit given our session binding implementation.

## Token Types

KnowOps uses JSON Web Tokens (JWT) for API authentication:

- **Access Token**: Used for authenticating individual API requests.
- **Refresh Token**: Used only to obtain a new access token.

## Token Lifetimes (Current)

| Token Type | Expiry | Algorithm |
|------------|--------|-----------|
| Access Token | **24 hours** | RS256 |
| Refresh Token | **30 days** | RS256 |

Access tokens now expire after **24 hours** (previously 1 hour). This change was approved by the security team and implemented with session binding (the token is tied to the originating IP and user agent). Refresh tokens are valid for 30 days (previously 7 days) to reduce the frequency of full re-authentication.

## Rationale for Change

- Developer ergonomics: Frequent 1-hour expiry caused CI pipelines to fail overnight
- Security compensating control: Session binding ties the token to originating context, reducing value of a stolen token
- Compliance: Internal policy allows up to 24-hour access tokens with session binding in place

## Storage and Security (Unchanged)

Access tokens: memory only (never localStorage). Refresh tokens: HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookies.

## Token Revocation

Tokens can be individually revoked (new capability added with this update) via `POST /auth/revoke` with the access token in the request body. Revoked tokens are added to a short-lived Redis blocklist checked on each request.

title: Kafka Topic Registry (v2 — Current)
content:
This document is the current authoritative registry of Kafka topics for the KnowOps platform.

**Note**: This page supersedes CONF-2006. All topic names were renamed in December 2025 as part of the Platform Naming Convention Migration (PCM-2025). The legacy topic names (events.raw, events.processed, etc.) from CONF-2006 no longer exist. Consumers and producers using old names will fail with 'Unknown topic' errors.

## Current Topic Inventory

| Topic Name | Purpose | Partitions | Retention |
|------------|---------|------------|----------|
| `platform.events.ingested` | Raw inbound events from all sources | 12 | 7 days |
| `platform.events.processed` | Events after enrichment and validation | 12 | 14 days |
| `platform.documents.ingest` | Documents queued for embedding | 6 | 3 days |
| `platform.documents.embedded` | Documents with computed vectors | 6 | 7 days |
| `platform.queries.submitted` | User queries received by API | 4 | 1 day |
| `platform.answers.generated` | Generated answers with sources | 4 | 3 days |
| `platform.documents.ingest.dlq` | Dead letter queue for failed embeds | 2 | 30 days |

## Naming Convention (Updated)

All topics now follow the pattern `{org}.{domain}.{stage}`. The previous pattern `{domain}.{stage}` without the org prefix is deprecated. Any new topics must use the `platform.` prefix.

## Migration from Legacy Names

If your consumer group is still using the old topic names from CONF-2006, update your configuration immediately. A migration tool is available:
```bash
python scripts/migrate_kafka_consumer_group.py --from events.raw --to platform.events.ingested
```

title: Embedding Model Configuration (Current — bge-m3 via Ollama)
content:
This document describes the current embedding model configuration for KnowOps.

**Note**: This page supersedes CONF-2007. The embedding model was migrated from OpenAI text-embedding-ada-002 (dim=1536, cloud API) to bge-m3 via Ollama (dim=1024, local CPU) in March 2026. Any configuration referencing text-embedding-ada-002 or dim=1536 is outdated and must not be used.

## Current Model

KnowOps uses **bge-m3** (BAAI General Embedding, multi-lingual, multi-granularity) served locally via **Ollama**. This model produces **1024-dimensional** vectors.

## API Configuration

```python
import httpx

OLLAMA_BASE_URL = os.environ.get("OLLAMA_BASE_URL", "http://ollama:11434")

def embed(text: str) -> list[float]:
    response = httpx.post(
        f"{OLLAMA_BASE_URL}/api/embeddings",
        json={"model": "bge-m3", "prompt": text},
        timeout=60.0
    )
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response.json()["embedding"]  # 1024-dimensional vector
```

## Milvus Collection Dimension

The Milvus vector field must be `dim=1024`. If you see a dimension mismatch error, the collection was likely created with the old dim=1536 for text-embedding-ada-002. Drop and recreate the collection after confirming all data has been re-embedded.

## Why We Migrated

- **Cost**: text-embedding-ada-002 cost $0.0001/1K tokens; bge-m3 via Ollama is free
- **Privacy**: No document content leaves the host machine
- **Latency**: Local embedding avoids network round-trip to OpenAI
- **Portability**: Portfolio project works without an OpenAI account

## Performance

- Single embedding: ~0.8s (CPU, Apple M-series or Intel)
- Output vectors are L2-normalized; use COSINE metric in Milvus

title: On-Call Escalation Policy (Revised — Current)
content:
This document defines the current on-call escalation policy for KnowOps production services.

**Note**: This page supersedes CONF-2008. The escalation path has been updated to include the Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) team as Tier 2 (replacing the previous secondary on-call engineer model), reflecting the team's growth and the introduction of a dedicated SRE function in Q1 2026.

## Escalation Tiers (Current)

### Tier 1 — On-Call Engineer
- Responds within **15 minutes** of alert firing.
- Responsible for initial triage, attempting first-line resolution, and keeping the incident thread updated.
- Rotation: weekly, Monday 09:00 UTC. Schedule in PagerDuty: KnowOps-Primary.

### Tier 2 — SRE Team (NEW)
- Escalate to the SRE on-call if incident is not resolved within **20 minutes** (previously 30) or requires infrastructure changes beyond application restart.
- SRE has elevated production access and can modify infrastructure configurations.
- PagerDuty: SRE-Primary schedule.

### Tier 3 — Engineering Manager + VP Engineering
- Escalate for P0 incidents persisting > **45 minutes** (previously 60) or any incident with confirmed data loss.
- VP Engineering must be notified for any P0 that is visible to customers.

## Communication Channels (Unchanged)

- PagerDuty alerts (automatic)
- #incidents Slack channel (post assessment within 10 minutes)
- Status page: P0 and P1 updates within 15 minutes

## Key Changes from CONF-2008

1. Tier 2 is now SRE on-call, not the engineering lead
2. Tier 2 escalation threshold reduced from 30 minutes to 20 minutes
3. Tier 3 escalation threshold reduced from 60 minutes to 45 minutes
4. VP Engineering added to Tier 3 notification

title: Glossary of RAG and Vector Search Terms
content:
This glossary defines key terms used throughout KnowOps documentation.

## RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)

A technique that combines a retrieval system (finding relevant documents) with a generative language model (producing a natural language answer). The retrieved documents are passed as context to the LLM, grounding the answer in factual source material.

## Vector Embedding

A numerical representation of text as a high-dimensional vector (e.g. 1024 dimensions). Semantically similar texts produce vectors that are close together in vector space, enabling similarity-based search.

## Cosine Similarity

A measure of similarity between two vectors based on the cosine of the angle between them. Returns 1.0 for identical direction, 0.0 for orthogonal. Used as the primary ranking signal in Milvus before recency reranking.

## HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World)

A graph-based vector index algorithm that enables approximate nearest-neighbor search in sub-linear time. KnowOps uses HNSW with M=16, ef_construction=200.

## Trap Pair

A KnowOps-specific term for a pair of documents that cover the same topic but contain contradictory information, with one being outdated and one correct. Used to evaluate recency-aware retrieval correctness.

## Recency Reranking

The process of adjusting retrieval rankings to favor more recently updated documents. In KnowOps, implemented as a formula combining cosine similarity score with an exponential decay function based on updated_date.

## Agentic Pipeline

A multi-step AI workflow where specialized agent modules (Planner, Retriever, Reranker, Answering Agent) each perform a distinct task in sequence, with structured data passed between them.

## Scalar Filter

A non-vector condition applied during Milvus search to restrict results by metadata field values (e.g. `source_type == 'jira'`, `priority == 'critical'`). Applied before vector similarity scoring.

title: Third-Party Service Dependency Map
content:
This document maps all third-party service dependencies for the KnowOps system.

## External Dependencies

| Service | Provider | Usage | Criticality | Fallback |
|---------|----------|-------|-------------|----------|
| LLM API | OpenRouter (Claude Sonnet) | Planner, Reranker, Answering Agent | Critical | None — pipeline fails if unavailable |
| Container Registry | Docker Hub | Image pull on deployment | High | Pre-pulled images on host |
| GitHub | GitHub.com | Source code, CI/CD | High | Local git mirror |

## Internal Dependencies (Docker)

| Service | Image Source | Criticality | Restart Policy |
|---------|-------------|-------------|----------------|
| Milvus | milvusdb/milvus | Critical | always |
| Etcd | quay.io/coreos/etcd | Critical | always |
| MinIO | minio/minio | Critical | always |
| Ollama | ollama/ollama | Critical | always |
| PostgreSQL | postgres:15 | Critical | always |

## Dependency Health Checks

The `/health` endpoint on the API service checks all internal dependencies:

```json
{
  "status": "ok",
  "checks": {
    "milvus": "ok",
    "postgres": "ok",
    "ollama": "ok",
    "openrouter": "ok"
  }
}
```

If any dependency is unhealthy, the API returns HTTP 503.

## SLA Impact

OpenRouter availability is the primary external SLA risk. OpenRouter's published SLA is 99.9% monthly uptime. If OpenRouter is unavailable, the entire pipeline is non-functional as there is no fallback LLM.

title: Security Threat Model for KnowOps
content:
This document summarizes the security threat model for the KnowOps system.

## Assets

1. **Indexed document content**: Jira tickets and Confluence pages (may contain internal business information)
2. **API keys**: OpenRouter key (billing impact if leaked)
3. **User query content**: May contain sensitive operational questions
4. **Generated answers**: May expose internal procedures if the system is accessible publicly

## Threat Actors

- **External attacker**: No direct access to Docker network; primary attack surface is the API endpoint.
- **Malicious internal user**: Could craft queries to extract indexed document content via the answering agent.
- **Compromised dependency**: Malicious code in a Docker image or Python dependency.

## Key Threats and Mitigations

| Threat | Mitigation |
|--------|------------|
| API key theft via environment | Keys only in .env (gitignored); never in source code |
| Prompt injection via user query | Input sanitization; answering agent instructed to ignore injection attempts |
| Unauthorized data access | JWT authentication required for all non-health endpoints |
| Document content exfiltration via LLM | Rate limiting; answer length limits; audit logging |
| Supply chain (malicious image) | Pin all Docker image digests; review Dockerfile dependencies |
| Exposed internal ports | Only API port 8080 exposed externally; all others localhost-only |

## Out of Scope (Portfolio Version)

- Data encryption at rest (not configured in development Docker Compose)
- Network segmentation beyond Docker bridge network
- WAF or API gateway
- Security scanning in CI (planned for production hardening)

title: Data Retention and Privacy Policy for KnowOps
content:
This document describes the data retention and privacy policy for KnowOps.

## Data Categories

| Category | Storage Location | Retention | Contains PII? |
|----------|-----------------|-----------|---------------|
| Indexed Jira tickets | Milvus + Postgres | Indefinite (until re-ingested) | Potentially (assignee names) |
| Indexed Confluence pages | Milvus + Postgres | Indefinite | Possibly (author names in content) |
| User queries | Logs (Loki) | 30 days | Potentially |
| Generated answers | Logs (Loki) | 30 days | Potentially |
| User feedback | Postgres | 1 year | No |
| Ingestion run records | Postgres | 1 year | No |

## PII Handling

Jira ticket 'assignee' fields contain real employee names. These are indexed and searchable in Milvus. Access to the KnowOps API must be restricted to authorized internal users only. Do not expose the KnowOps API to the public internet without a legal review of PII exposure implications.

## Data Subject Requests

For GDPR/CCPA data subject requests:
1. Identify all Jira tickets and Confluence pages authored by or assigned to the individual.
2. Delete or anonymize the relevant records in the source Jira/Confluence system.
3. Re-run the ingestion pipeline to update KnowOps indexes.
4. Confirm log retention compliance: logs older than 30 days are automatically purged.

## Data Locality

All data is stored on-premises (or on a company-controlled cloud VM). No document content is sent to any external service except the query context sent to OpenRouter for LLM calls. OpenRouter's data processing agreement must be reviewed before deploying with real internal data.
